coredata.dc:title: Up-converted nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots to accelerate charge transfer of dibismuth tetraoxide for enhanced full-spectrum photocatalytic activity
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Zheng, Shizheng
Qin, Feng
Hu, Changyuan
Zhang, Cuiqing
Ma, Yanting
Yang, Ruofan
Wei, Lin
Bao, Liwen
coredata.dc:description: The charge carriers’ separation and the light harvesting ability are two key factors affecting the photocatalytic activity of photocatalysts. In this work, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) modified Bi2O4 (N-CQDs/Bi2O4) are fabricated via a facile hydrothermal method, which serve as efficient photocatalysts toward methyl orange (MO) degradation. The photocatalytic experimental results demonstrate that enhanced photocatalytic activities are acquired for all N-CQDs modified Bi2O4 composites. Moreover, 0.3 % N-CQDs/Bi2O4 exhibits the optimal photocatalytic activity with the reaction rate about 3.1-fold, 2.3-fold, and 2.9-fold of pure Bi2O4 under solar light, visible light, and near-infrared light irradiation. Such significantly increased photocatalytic performance can be ascribed to the following two advantages of N-CQDs: high conductivity that accelerate the separation of charge carriers and the upconversion behavior to enlarge light harvesting range. Additionally, the trapping experiments suggest that holes (h+) are of vital importance for the degradation process. This work paves the way for developing advanced photocatalyst with high charge separation efficiency and full-spectrum utilization.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bi2O4
N-CQDs
Photocatalyst
Upconversion
Charge separation

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Illustration of synthesizing N-CQDs/Bi2O4 hybrid, (b-d) TEM images of synthesized N-CQDs, (e) XRD pattern of as-synthesized N-CQDs.
Optical photographs of N-CQDs aqueous (a) and photoluminescence (b), PL spectra of N-CQDs excited by different wavelength (c).
XRD patterns of Bi2O4 and N-CQDs/Bi2O4 hybrids with various N-CQDs contents (0.05 %-0.5 %).
SEM (a, b) and TEM (c, d) images of as prepared samples: (a, c) Bi2O4, (b, d) 0.3 %N-CQDs/Bi2O4, and (e) EDS mapping of Bi, O, C and N of 0.3 %N-CQDs/Bi2O4 hybrid.
XPS spectra of Bi2O4 and 0.3 % N-CQDs/Bi2O4 (a) survey spectra, (b) Bi 4f, (c) O 1 s and (d) C 1 s for the 0.3 % N-CQDs/Bi2O4 composite.

(a) UV–vis optical absorption spectroscopy of Bi2O4 and N-CQDs/Bi2O4 materials with different N-CQDs content. (b) The amplified optical absorption at 600-800 nm. (c) Transient photocurrent responses and (d) electrochemical impedance spectra of Bi2O4 and N-CQDs/Bi2O4 under visible light (λ > 420 nm) irradiation at 0 V versus SEC. [Na2SO4] = 0.5 M.

Photocatalytic degradation of MO in the presence of Bi2O4 and N-CQDs/Bi2O4 composites under (a)solar, (c)visible and (e)NIR light irradiation; The Pseudo-first-order kinetic curves of the photocatalytic degradation of MO in the presence of Bi2O4 and N-CQDs/Bi2O4 composites under (b)solar, (d)visible and (f)NIR light irradiation.

(a) Trapping experiment of active species during the photocatalytic degradation of MO over 0.3 % N-CQDs/Bi2O4 under visible light irradiation, (b) Spectral changes of MO over 0.3 % N-CQDs/Bi2O4 composite.
Schematic photocatalytic mechanism for the N-CQDs/Bi2O4 composites under solar, visible and NIR light irradiation.

head.ce:title: Up-converted nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots to accelerate charge transfer of dibismuth tetraoxide for enhanced full-spectrum photocatalytic activity

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Jiangxi Science and Technology Normal University, Nanchang, 330038, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: cSchool of Pharmacy, Jiangxi Science and Technology Normal University, Nanchang 330038, P. R. China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this paper.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Up-converted nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) were utilized to modify Bi2O4 to construct advanced N-CQDs/Bi2O4 photocatalyst. The enhanced full spectrum photocatalytic activity is derived from the following two advantages of N-CQDs: high conductivity that accelerate the separation of charge carriers and the upconversion behavior to enlarge light harvesting range.

The charge carriers’ separation and the light harvesting ability are two key factors affecting the photocatalytic activity of photocatalysts. In this work, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) modified Bi2O4 (N-CQDs/Bi2O4) are fabricated via a facile hydrothermal method, which serve as efficient photocatalysts toward methyl orange (MO) degradation. The photocatalytic experimental results demonstrate that enhanced photocatalytic activities are acquired for all N-CQDs modified Bi2O4 composites. Moreover, 0.3 % N-CQDs/Bi2O4 exhibits the optimal photocatalytic activity with the reaction rate about 3.1-fold, 2.3-fold, and 2.9-fold of pure Bi2O4 under solar light, visible light, and near-infrared light irradiation. Such significantly increased photocatalytic performance can be ascribed to the following two advantages of N-CQDs: high conductivity that accelerate the separation of charge carriers and the upconversion behavior to enlarge light harvesting range. Additionally, the trapping experiments suggest that holes (h+) are of vital importance for the degradation process. This work paves the way for developing advanced photocatalyst with high charge separation efficiency and full-spectrum utilization.

ce:section.ce:para: Driven by the worsening environmental crises (such as water and gas pollution), exploring clean and sustainable strategies to relieve these crises are highly desired [1]. Photocatalysis, which can utilize in-exhaustible solar light as energy source to decompose contaminations, has triggered great attention [2]. As well known, the photocatalytic efficiency of photocatalyst is greatly influenced by the charge carriers' behaviors and the light harvesting properties [3]. In this regard, exploring photocatalysts with wide solar light response range and fast electron-holes separation is of great significance for increasing the photocatalytic activity.

In recent years, bismuth-based semiconductors, such as NaBiO3 [4], Bi2O3 [5,6], BiOX [7], BiVO4 [8], Bi2O2CO3 [9] and Bi2O4 [10,11], have triggered great attention due to their high photodegradation efficiency toward organic contaminants. On account of the superiority of unique mixed valence electron structure and narrow band gap (2.0 eV), dibismuth tetraoxide (Bi2O4) is considered as promising visible-light-driven photocatalyst [12–14]. Nevertheless, the photoactivity of pristine Bi2O4 is still hindered by the rapid recombination of photogenerated electron–hole pairs. Enormous efforts have been undertaken to overcome such drawback by means of constructing heterojunctions (such as g-C3N4/Bi2O4 [15,16], BiOX/Bi2O4 [17,18], Bi2O3/Bi2O4 [14,19] and Bi2O4/TiO2 [20–23]). Beneficial from the promoted electron-hole separation on build-in interfaces, these Bi2O4-based heterojunctions displayed increased photocatalytic activity toward dye removal. Despite these successes, the photocatalytic performance of Bi2O4 is still restricted by some unsettled problems. On the one hand, semiconductors were selected as the counterparts to create heterostructures, whose poor conductivity would go against the charge transfer. On the other hand, only UV and visible light can be captured by these heterostructures, which account for less than 50 % of the full solar spectrum. Therefore, how to modify Bi2O4 with more improved charge transfer and further broadened light harvesting range remains challenging.

Hybridizing semiconductors with carbons (CNTs, graphene, carbon quantum dots (CQDs), et al) has been proven as an effective avenue to not only promote charge transfer but also improve the optical absorbance, and thus increase the photocatalytic activity [24–26]. CQDs, a new zero-dimensional carbon material with small particle size (<5 nm), have triggered intense attention in photocatalysis due to the excellent physical and chemical properties [27–30]. Especially, the up-converted photoluminescence property endowed CQDs converting longer wavelength radiation into shorter wavelength fluorescence [31]. As such, great endeavors have been taken to hybrid CQDs with semiconductors (CeO2 [32], Fe2O3 [33] and TiO2 [34]) in case of improving the photocatalytic performance under the irradiation of visible and NIR light. Compared to pure CQDs, N-doped CQDs rich with surface-state defects and new excited energy potentials display improved fluorescence quantum yields and inherent properties [29]. Very recently, Yu and co-authors fabricated N-CQDs decorated BiO2-x via depositing N-CQDs onto BiO2-x nanosheets, which displayed enhanced photocatalytic efficiency under full-spectrum, visible and NIR light irradiations [31]. Such enhancement is derived from the following factors: on the one hand, charge transfer is greatly promoted due to the high conductivity of N-CQDs; on the other hand, the solar spectrum harvesting is extended to NIR region result from the upconversion behavior of N-CQDs. Due to the BiO2-x is oxygen species-dominated photocatalyst, activating molecular oxygen to generate more reactive oxygen species seems to be the key point. On the contrary, Bi2O4 has been proved as hole-dominated photocatalyst, and whether CQDs decoration is effective for improving the photocatalytic activity of Bi2O4 still need further investigation.

Herein, full solar spectrum responded N-CQDs decorated Bi2O4 photocatalysts were prepared via a facile hydrothermal process. The PL spectra confirm the up-conversion behavior of N-CQDs and the TEM results reveal the combination between N-CQDs and Bi2O4. The photocatalytic activities of the as-prepared photocatalysts were evaluated by the degradation of methyl orange (MO) in aqueous solution under visible, solar and NIR light irradiation. Importantly, 0.3 %N-CQDs/Bi2O4 composites showed significantly improved photocatalytic activities under solar and visible light irradiation in comparison with pure Bi2O4 and exhibited NIR photocatalytic activity. N-CQDs not only serve as an intermedium to promote charge carrier’s separation, but also photo centers to convert NIR light to visible light, and thus improve the photocatalytic performance of Bi2O4. The trapping experiment suggests that holes play vital roles in the degradation process and a possible mechanism was posed. This work paves the way for developing rapid surface charge transferring and highly efficient solar light harvesting photocatalyst.

Sodium bismuthate (NaBiO3·2H2O), sodium oxalate (Na2C2O4), urea (CO(NH2)2), benzoquinone (BQ), isopropanol (IPA), sodium sulfate (Na2SO4) and methyl orange (MO) were analytical reagents and used without further purification. Ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ
cm−1) was used for all the experiments.

The N-CQDs were firstly prepared following the previously reported method [35]. In a typical synthesis, Na2C2O4 and CO(NH2)2 were homogeneously dispersed in 12 mL DI water and then transferred to 50 mL autoclave heating for 8 h in 160 ℃ ovens. After that, the resultants were centrifugated to remove the large particles and then purified via semipermeable membrane. A certain content of NaBiO3 and 60 mL N-CQDs aqueous dispersion were mixed as raw materials to synthesize N-CQDs/Bi2O4 composites. The above mixture was stirred for 10 min, and then transferred into a 100 mL Teflon lined stainless autoclave and heated at 160 ℃ for 12 h. After collected by filtration and washed three times with DI water and ethanol, respectively, the products were dried at 60 ℃ overnight. The obtained samples were denoted as x% N-CQDs/Bi2O4 according to the content of N-CQDs in the composites. For comparison, pristine Bi2O4 was prepared under the same conditions using NaBiO3 as raw material without the addition of N-CQDs.

Field emission scanning electron microscopy (Zeiss, Sigma) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM 2100) were used to investigate the morphology and microstructure. Rigaku X-ray diffractometer was applied to obtain crystallite information using Cu Kα as radiation. The UV–vis absorbance spectra were obtained using a UV–vis spectrophotometer (Perkin Elmer Lambda 35) ranged from UV to visible light (300−800 nm). We use the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, KRATOS Analytical AXISHSi spectrometer) to measure the composition and chemical status of synthesized photocatalysts with a monochromatized Al Ka X-ray source (1486.6 eV photons). Photoelectrochemical measurements were performed in an electrochemical workstation (CHI-660E instruments).

Methyl orange (MO) is selected as the targeted molecules to evaluate the photocatalytic activity of as-synthesized photocatalysts under light irradiation. Typically, the concentration of as-prepared photocatalysts were about 0.4 mg/mL in 25 mg/L MO aqueous solutions. Prior to irradiation, an adsorption–desorption equilibrium of the suspensions was achieved via stirring for an hour in dark. After that, the suspensions were exposed to different light sources and at regular time intervals, the suspensions were collected and centrifuged to remove the photocatalyst. Then the concentration of as-collected MO dye was identified according to the absorption recorded from the UV–vis spectra. In order to identify the active species during photocatalysis process, the scavenger quenching experiments were executed. Typically, sodium oxalate (2 mM), isopropyl alcohol (2 mM) and p-benzoquinone (BQ, 0.2 mM) were respectively used to capture holes (h+), hydroxyl radicals (
OH) and superoxide radical (
O2 −).

Photoelectrochemical measurements were performed in a typical three-electrode system in Na2SO4 solution (0.5 M) using platinum wire and saturated calomel electrode (SCE) as the counter and the reference electrode, respectively. The Bi2O4 and 0.3 % N-CQDs/Bi2O4 composite on FTO were served as the working electrodes. The visible light (λ > 420 nm) photocurrent of photocatalysts were carried out at 0.0 V via light on and off. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were performed in the frequency range from 10−2 Hz to 105 Hz.

As illustrated in Fig. 1 a, the N-CQDs decorated Bi2O4 were synthesized via two-step hydrothermal method. Typically, the N-CQDs were prepared using Na2C2O4 and urea as starting materials [35]. Firstly, the morphology and microstructure of N-CQDs was characterized by TEM. As shown in Fig. 1b, the N-CQDs appear to be uniform dot shape and dispersed well, which is consistent with the results in previous reports [36]. The further amplified TEM images (Fig. 1c and d) demonstrate the uniform distribution of N-CQDs diameter, whose size is calculated to less than 5 nm. Thanks to such small particle size, efficient contact between N-CQDs and semiconductors will be guaranteed, and thus result in the promoted electron transfer from semiconductors to N-CQDs. Moreover, beneficial from quantum confinement effects of such sized N-CQDs, upconvertion behavior is probable displayed, which can enlarge the response range of semiconductors [37]. To demonstrate the phase structure of as-collected N-CQDs powder, XRD was executed and the results are displayed in Fig. 1e. As can be seen, N-CQDs exhibit a wide diffraction peak at 2θ = 20°∼30°, belonging to the diffraction peak of carbon. As expected, the intensity of the diffraction peak is weak, indicating the low crystallinity of N-CQDs [38].

To further explore the optical properties of N-CQDs, photoluminescence (PL) of the as-prepared N-CQDs was carried out. As clearly displayed in Fig. 2a–b, N-CQDs aqueous can emit photoluminescence when exposed to light. To directly determine the upconvertion effect of N-CQDs, light with various excitation wavelengths were utilized to driven N-CQDs and the results are shown in Fig. 2c. Obviously, al-most all the emission peaks located at visible light range from 400 to 550 nm, which is shorter than the corresponding excitation wavelengths. Namely, the longer excitation wavelengths are converted to shorter emission wavelengths due to the upconvertion effect of N-CQDs. Moreover, with the increase of excitation wavelength changes from 550 to1000 nm, the emission peak intensity is increased first and then decreased gradually, reaching the maximum emission at excitation wavelength of 750 nm. Notably, under the excitation wavelength of 750 nm, highest PL emission peak centered at about 480 nm can be distinguished, which is much smaller than the light absorption edge of pristine Bi2O4(∼ 600 nm). These results suggest that N-CQDs possess obvious up-conversion behavior that can convert NIR light to visible light ranged from 400 to 550 nm.

As described in Fig. 1a, N-CQDs/Bi2O4 composites were synthesized via a facile hydrothermal strategy, using NaBiO3 and a certain of N-CQDs as raw materials. To ascertain whether the phase structure of Bi2O4 was changed after N-CQDs modification, the XRD patterns of as-prepared pristine Bi2O4 and N-CQDs/Bi2O4 photocatalysts were executed and are shown in Fig. 3 . Evidently, well crystallized pristine Bi2O4 with strong diffraction peaks at 2θ = 18.9, 26.8, 29.5, 30.3, 32.5, 35.0 and 47.0° is seen, which can be ascribed to the monoclinic phase of Bi2O4 (JCPDS NO.83-0410) [15]. With respect to the N-CQDs/Bi2O4 composites, similar XRD patterns are observed compared with pristine Bi2O4, and no other diffraction peaks of impurities were detected. Worth noting that no diffraction peaks of N-CQDs are detected, which probably ascribed to the low content of N-CQDs or the shielding effect of well-crystallized Bi2O4. Therefore, it is confirmable that introducing N-CQDs has negligible influence on the crystal structures of Bi2O4.

Further, in order to reveal the changes of microstructure after N-CQDs modification, the morphologies of the pristine Bi2O4 and 0.3 %N-CQDs/Bi2O4 composite were characterized by SEM and TEM. As shown in Fig. 4(a, b), the pristine Bi2O4 and 0.3 % N-CQDs/Bi2O4 exhibit resemble rod-like morphology with diameter ranging from 300 nm to 500 nm and the length about 2−4 μm. This result confirms that introducing N-CQDs during hydrothermal process cannot influence the morphology of Bi2O4. However, further TEM images (Fig. 4c–d) reveal that after N-CQDs decoration the smooth surface of pristine Bi2O4 turns to rough and some tiny nanoparticles are observed anchoring on the surface of rod-shaped Bi2O4, which probably ascribed to the N-CQDs. To directly confirm the presence of N-CQDs in as-prepared hybrid photocatalyst, the EDS mapping of 0.3 %N-CQDs/Bi2O4 was then carried out and the results is displayed in Fig. 4e. Obviously, elemental Bi, O, C and N are detected and uniformly dispersed in the hybrid, confirming the successful combination of N-CQDs and Bi2O4.

To further understand the surface composition and chemical status of Bi2O4 and N-CQDs/Bi2O4 hybrid, the XPS measurement was carried out and the results are shown in Fig. 5 . As described in Fig. 5a, both Bi, O and C elements were appeared in Bi2O4 and 0.3 % N-CQDs/Bi2O4, and the unexpected C 1s signal in Bi2O4 is probably derived from the carbon-based contaminant from vacuum system [23]. Moreover, compared with pure Bi2O4, the intensity of C 1s peak in 0.3 % N-CQDs/Bi2O4 hybrid is slightly higher, which confirms the exist of N-CQDs in hybrid. In addition, no N 1s signal is observed in the spectrum, which may be due to its low content in N-CQDs/Bi2O4 hybrid. Clearly, the high-resolution Bi 4f spectrum of Bi2O4 and 0.3 %N-CQDs/Bi2O4 can be well deconvoluted into two bimodal peaks (Fig. 5b). For pure Bi2O4, the binding energies of 158.3 and 159.3 eV for Bi 4f7/2, and 163.5 and 164.7 eV for Bi 4f5/2 are displayed, confirming the presence of Bi (III) and Bi(V) in Bi2O4 [35]. Similarly, the orbital binding energies of Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2 of 0.3 % N-CQDs/Bi2O4 are located at 158.6 eV (159.6 eV) and 163.8 eV (165.1 eV), respectively. Specifically, compared to pristine Bi2O4, the Bi 4f orbital binding energies of 0.3 % N-CQDs/Bi2O4 show an obvious positive shift, revealing that electrons of Bi2O4 were transferred to N-CQDs [22]. The O 1s high-resolution spectra of Bi2O4 and 0.3 % N-CQDs/Bi2O4 can be deconvoluted into three peaks (Fig. 5c), ascribing to lattice O of Bi2O4, adsorbed oxygen (H2O) and surface hydroxyl groups (OH−), respectively. Notably, the binding energy of lattice O in 0.3 % N-CQDs/Bi2O4 shows a positive shift towards higher binding energy, which is consistent with the shift direction of Bi 4f peak. These results further suggest the exist of intense interaction between Bi2O4 and N-CQDs. As displayed in Fig. 5d, the C 1s peak of 0.3 % N-CQDs/Bi2O4 can be deconvoluted into three peaks, where the one peak at 284.6 eV is attributed to the CC bond with a sp2 orbital and the other two peaks at 286.3 and 288.6 eV are belonged to the CN and CO bonds, respectively [39]. These results further prove that the N-CQDs have been successfully anchored on the surface of Bi2O4, forming strong electronic coupled interaction for separating the photogenerated electron-hole pairs.

UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) were recorded to explore the light absorption ability of Bi2O4 and N-CQDs/Bi2O4 composites. As seen from Fig. 6 a, the light-absorption edge of pristine Bi2O4 can be extended to 595 nm, corresponding to the band gap of about 2.10 eV, which is consistent well with the previous reports [11,40]. With respect to the N-CQDs/Bi2O4 composites, the light-absorption edges display no significant change indeed, however, an enhanced absorption tails ranged from 650 nm to 800 nm can be observed (Fig. 6b). The enhanced Vis-NIR light absorption ability of as-synthesized composites is beneficial for the improvement of the photocatalytic activity [16], which is consistent with the photocatalytic experiment results in Fig. 7 .

Apart from optical properties, electron-hole pairs separation efficiency also plays key roles in affecting the photocatalytic performance. To reveal the separation properties of charge carriers, electrochemical measurements including photocurrent response and EIS were carried out. The photocurrent test is a well-known strategy to reflect the separation and transfer efficiency of the photo-generated carrier under light irradiation. Hereby, photocurrent response test on pristine Bi2O4 and 0.3 % N-CQDs/Bi2O4 under visible light irradiation was conducted as an example. As can be seen in Fig. 6c, Bi2O4 exhibits weaker photocurrent response due to the fast recombination of photogenerated carriers, while the photocurrent intensity of 0.3 % N-CQDs/Bi2O4 is about 1.5 times higher than that of pristine Bi2O4. Such an enhanced photocurrent response indicates an improvement in the separation and transfer efficiency of the photo-generated h+/e− pairs, which is in accordance with the photocatalytic performance shown below. In addition, EIS is also a powerful electrochemical technique to determine the kinetics of charge transfer via the arc in the high frequency section. As shown in the Nyquist plots (Fig. 6d), compared to Bi2O4, an obviously decreased diameter of arc is seen for 0.3 %N-CQDs/Bi2O4, which indicate a relative smaller charge transfer resistance in 0.3 %N-CQDs/Bi2O4. Namely, the EIS spectra results revealed that the 0.3 %N-CQDs/Bi2O4 possess a higher charge carrier transfer efficiency than Bi2O4.

The photocatalytic activity of Bi2O4 and N-CQDs/Bi2O4 composites were evaluated for the degradation of organic dyes (MO) under solar, visible and NIR light irradiation, and the results were exhibited in Fig. 7a–f. As shown in Fig. 7a, under solar light irradiation, about 65 % MO molecules were removed within 75 min using pristine Bi2O4 as photocatalyst. Apparently, after N-CQDs decoration, the N-CQDs/Bi2O4 composites exhibit superior photocatalytic performance than Bi2O4 and with the increase content of N-CQDs, the photocatalytic activity increased gradually. As can be seen, when the content of N-CQDs is up to 0.3 %, optimal photocatalytic activity was achieved. With further increase the content of N-CQDs, the photocatalytic activity no longer enhanced, which probably ascribed to the excessive N-CQDs that hinder the light absorption [41]. To directly reflect the photocatalytic efficiency, the photocatalytic reaction rates are fitted to the pseudo-first-order kinetic and display in Fig. 7b. According to the pseudo-first-order kinetic curves, the rate constant of 0.3 % N-CQDs/Bi2O4 is calculated to 0.04025 min−1, about 2.12 times higher than that of pristine Bi2O4 (0.01285 min−1). Similarly, under visible light irradiation, N-CQDs modified Bi2O4 composites display resemble photodegradation regular as under solar light irradiation with the best N-CQDs/Bi2O4 composite obtained at the N-CQDs content of 0.3 % (Fig. 7c). Moreover, the pseudo-first-order kinetic curves (Fig. 7d) reveal that the rate constant of 0.3 % N-CQDs/Bi2O4 is 2.18 times of pristine Bi2O4. According to the experimental results, after N-CQDs modification, Bi2O4-based composites displayed enhanced photocatalytic performance under both visible and solar light irradiation. Such enhancement is derived from the excellent electron conductivity of N-CQDs, which can work as the electron reservoir to accelerate the photogenerated charge carrier’s separation.

To further demonstrate whether N-CQDs/Bi2O4 composite have full solar spectrum photocatalytic performance, the photocatalytic activity of N-CQDs/Bi2O4 composite under NIR light were also executed. Obviously, for pristine Bi2O4, under NIR light irradiation up to 300 min, only 30 % MO molecules were removed (Fig. 7e). However, after forming N-CQDs/Bi2O4 composites, remarkably enhanced photocatalytic performance is displayed and optimal photocatalytic activity is also achieved at N-CQDs content of 0.3 %. As expected, 0.3 %N-CQDs/Bi2O4 composite shows the highest rate constant of 0.00362 min−1, which is about 3 times of pristine Bi2O4 (Fig. 7f). The enhanced NIR photocatalytic activity should be attributed to the up-converted behavior of N-CQDs which can convert NIR light and to visible light to excite Bi2O4, and subsequently NIR-photocatalytic performance is obtained.

Demonstrating the active species during photocatalysis is of vital importance for revealing the degradation mechanism, and thus free radicals trapping experiments of N-CQDs/Bi2O4 composites were further performed. According to the literature, Na2C2O4, IPA and BQ are common scavengers to respectively capture holes (h+), hydroxyl radicals (
OH) and superoxide radicals (
O2 −) [11,23]. As shown in Fig. 8 a, under visible light irradiation, the removal efficiency of 0.3 % N-CQDs/Bi2O4 composite is about 85 % without scavengers. Similarly, with the introduction of IPA, slightly photodegradation efficiency decay is visualized, illustrating that hydroxyl radicals have a negligible effect on the photocatalytic activity. However, obvious performance decay is seen after the introduction of BQ, which suggests that the superoxide radicals are of certain importance for the photocatalytic reactions. Additionally, when Na2C2O4 was added into the system, the photocatalytic activity was significantly inhibited and only 10 % MO molecules were removed, revealing that holes play a crucial role in photodegradation process. Based on the results mentioned above, both superoxide radicals and holes are taking part in the photocatalytic reactions but holes are the main active species. These results are consisting well with the results reported in previous Bi2O4-based photocatalysts [11,23].

To reveal the mechanism of MO degradation, 0.3 % N-CQDs/Bi2O4 composite were selected as the target to decompose MO under visible light irradiation. The intensities of absorption spectra of MO were investigated, and the results are displayed in Fig. 8b. As shown in the graph inserted in Fig. 8b, the MO solution obviously changed from yellow to colorless, revealing the occurrence of photodegradation reaction. The absorption spectra further confirm the photodegradation mechanism of MO. With the irradiation time goes on, the intensities of absorption spectra centered at 464.2 nm decrease gradually, and no new peaks emerged. Such phenomenon confirms that the mechanism of MO degradation is not a decolorization process, but a minimization step to produce CO2 and H2O [18].

Based on the above experimental and characteristic results, the roles of N-CQDs under visible and NIR light irradiation are summarized and deeply discussed. Detailed explanations are as follows: Firstly, N-CQDs can function as electron collector and transporter to trap and transfer electrons generated from Bi2O4, hence enhancing the charge separation and photocatalytic performance. Secondly, N-CQDs can convert longer wavelength to shorter wavelength due to the upconvertion behavior, which widen the light absorption and results in more photoinduced charges. According to the discussion mentioned above, a possible photocatalytic mechanism over N-CQDs/Bi2O4 composite is proposed, as illustrated in Fig. 9 . With visible light irradiation, Bi2O4, which serve as the photoresponse center, can be excited and generate electron-hole pairs. The electrons on the conductive band can migrate to the surface of Bi2O4 due to the excellent conductivity of N-CQDs and then captured by the absorbed O2 to form ∙O2 −, which can oxidize MO to H2O and CO2. Simultaneously, the oxidative holes left on the value band can directly oxidize MO to H2O and CO2. Based on the previous trapping experiments, hole oxidation process is the main reaction pathway due to its relative strong oxide ability. However, the photocatalytic mechanism under NIR light irradiation is quite different from that under visible light, due to the low energy of NIR light. Under NIR light irradiation, the NIR light can be captured and up-converted to visible light due to the existence of N-doped band in N-CQDs. According to the analysis talked above, the synergistic of the upconversion effect and excellent conductivity of N-CQDs resulted in remarkable photocatalytic activity of N-CQDs/Bi2O4 under NIR light irradiation.

In summary, N-CQDs modified Bi2O4 composites have been successfully constructed as an efficient photocatalyst via one-step hydrothermal method. The N-CQDs/Bi2O4 photocatalyst exhibits excellent photocatalytic activity for the degradation of methylene orange (MO) under different light sources. Under the solar, visible, and near-infrared irradiation, 0.3 % N-CQDs/Bi2O4 shows the optimal removal efficiency toward MO, which is about 3.1, 2.3 and 2.9 times higher than pristine Bi2O4 respectively. The enhanced photocatalytic performance is derived from the following two advantages of N-CQDs: a) high conductivity that accelerate the separation of charge carriers; b) the upconversion behavior to enlarge light harvesting range. A probable photodegradation mechanism is posed based on the trapping experiments. This work paves the way for developing advanced photocatalyst with rapid surface charge transferring and highly efficient solar light harvesting.

Shizheng Zheng: Writing - original draft, Methodology. Feng Qin: Data curation, Writing - review & editing. Changyuan Hu: Supervision, Writing - review & editing. Cuiqing Zhang: Conceptualization, Supervision. Yanting Ma: Visualization. Ruofan Yang: Visualization. Lin Wei: Visualization. Liwen Bao: Visualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors report no declarations of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to express their thanks for the support of National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21663012), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangxi Province (20181BAB203009), and Scientific and Technological Project of Education Department of Jiangxi Province (No. GJJ201129 and No. GJJ201132). This work was also financially supported by the Doctoral Start-up Fund (No. 2019BSQD002), and Innovation & Entrepreneurship Project of Jiangxi Science and Technology Normal University, P.R. China (No. S202011318048).
